result.summary: OpenObserve exposes a tenant-specific HTTP API and an MCP server for AI clients; both require authentication, with documented Basic auth and additional service-account/token-based API access mentioned for programmatic use.
result.description: OpenObserve is an open source observability platform for logs, metrics, traces, and real user monitoring. It can be deployed self-hosted or used as a managed cloud service, and uses SQL and PromQL over telemetry data.

oo_basic.label: OpenObserve user credentials (email/user ID + password) for HTTP Basic auth
oo_basic.setup: Sign in to your OpenObserve Cloud or self-hosted OpenObserve instance and use your account's user ID/email plus password to build an HTTP Basic `Authorization` header. The API reference says all APIs require an authorization header created from base64-encoded user ID and password using HTTP Basic auth. For hosted Cloud, start from [OpenObserve Cloud login](https://cloud.openobserve.ai).

oo_service_account_token.label: OpenObserve service account token
oo_service_account_token.setup: In OpenObserve, go to IAM → Service Accounts, click `Add Service Account`, enter the required details, then copy the token shown after creation. Assign permissions via IAM → Roles as needed. The service account blog describes these tokens as the secure way to access APIs programmatically without sharing user credentials.

surfaces.name: OpenObserve HTTP API
surfaces.notes: Docs state all APIs require an authorization header and describe HTTP Basic auth using base64-encoded user ID and password. A later blog states service account tokens provide programmatic API access for non-ingestion APIs, but the exact HTTP binding format was not fully visible in the retrieved content. OpenObserve supports both managed Cloud and self-hosted deployments, so the API host is instance-specific.

surfaces.name: OpenObserve MCP server

variables.name: organization
variables.resolveFrom: OpenObserve org name in the MCP URL path; docs say each organization has its own MCP endpoint.
variables.description: Organization slug/path segment replacing `default` for non-default orgs.

surfaces.notes: The marketing and blog pages describe direct client connection via stdio/SSE, but the concrete endpoint example shown is an HTTP MCP URL used with `claude mcp add ... -t http`. Docs also state each organization has its own MCP endpoint, so the example `default` path is organization-specific.
